name: CIL
summary: .NET's stack-based, typed, verifiable bytecode (ECMA-335). Shares the JVM's stack-based shape; adds explicit value types, reified generics, unsafe pointer types, and tail-call instructions. JIT-compiled by RyuJIT.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
CIL is .NET's bytecode, sharing the JVM's stack-based, typed, verifiable shape (ECMA-335). The deltas worth recording are what CIL adds beyond JVM: explicit value types (structs that don't heap-allocate), reified generics (preserved through CIL rather than erased), unsafe pointer types, and tail-call instructions.

The runtime (CoreCLR) JIT-compiles CIL via RyuJIT. The design philosophy is "the runtime is a target, not a translator": source languages (C#, F#, VB.NET, IronPython, IronRuby) compile to CIL with as little semantic loss as possible, and the runtime JITs the features rather than the front-end lowering them away.

sections.title: Value types and reified generics
sections.content:
CIL's distinctive features over JVM bytecode are value types and reified generics. A `struct` in C# allocates on the stack or inline in containing objects rather than heap-allocating; CIL preserves that distinction through to the JIT, which can flatten value-type fields into surrounding storage.

Reified generics keep type parameters in the CIL metadata at runtime: `List<int>` is a distinct type from `List<string>` at the bytecode level. This is the opposite of Java's erasure, and is the reason Project Valhalla on the JVM is a multi-year retrofit — what CIL got at design time, the JVM is recovering decades later.
